title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Terminologies used in Plankton Ecology
Plankton systematics

The study of the classification and taxonomy of planktonic organisms.
It involves identifying and categorizing different species of plankton based on their morphological, physiological, ecological, and genetic characteristics of planktonic organisms.
This field helps us understand the diversity and evolutionary relationships among planktonic species, as well as their distribution patterns and ecological roles in aquatic ecosystems.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Terminologies…
Plankton ecology

Plankton ecology is the study of planktonic organisms and their interactions with each other and their environment within aquatic ecosystems.
It includes the distribution, abundance, diversity, behavior, physiology, and ecological roles of plankton.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Terminologies…
Plankton productivity

Plankton productivity refers to the rate at which planktonic organisms produce organic matter through photosynthesis and other metabolic processes.
It is a critical component of aquatic ecosystems, as plankton are the primary producers that form the base of the food web and support the entire ecosystem.
Understanding plankton productivity is essential for understanding the dynamics of aquatic ecosystems and the impacts of human activities on these ecosystems.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
What is Phytoplankton?

Phytoplankton is derived from the Greek words:

Phyto – (plant)
Plankton — (wanderer or drifter)

Phytoplankton are microscopic aquatic plants which do not have ability to swim
They are free-floating aquatic organisms that depend entirely on water movement such as surface currents for their movement.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phytoplankton…

They thrive in euphotic zone; the upper water column of the world’s oceans, lakes, rivers, ponds.
The euphotic zone receives energy from the sun that allows phytoplankton to photosynthesize – a chemical reaction that converts light energy into chemical energy (Peter et al., 2018).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Phytoplankton…

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
What is Phytoplankton Ecology?

Definition: Study of phytoplankton organisms and their interactions with each other and their aquatic environment
Key focus areas:

Distribution and abundance patterns
Physiological responses to environmental factors
Population dynamics and growth rates
Ecological roles in food webs and nutrient cycling
Community structure and biodiversity

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
section: Photosynthesis: The Heart of Phytoplankton Ecology
text:
Photosynthesis: The Heart of Phytoplankton Ecology



Phytoplankton contain Chlorophyll-a (Chl-a) – a green pigment responsible for photosynthesis
Chl-a captures light energy and converts it into chemical energy (glucose)
This is the foundation of all aquatic food webs


Key products:

Organic matter (glucose) - food for consumers
Oxygen (O₂) - byproduct for respiration
Accounts for ~50% of Earth’s oxygen production

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Photosynthesis…
The Photosynthetic Equation:
\[6CO_2 + 6H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Sunlight + Chlorophyll}} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2\]

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Primary Productivity

Definition: Rate at which phytoplankton produce organic matter through photosynthesis
Two types:

Gross Primary Productivity (GPP): Total organic matter produced
Net Primary Productivity (NPP): Organic matter remaining after phytoplankton respiration

Global significance: Phytoplankton account for ~50% of total organic matter production on Earth

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors Controlling Phytoplankton Growth
1. Light (Photosynthetically Active Radiation - PAR)

PAR is the portion of sunlight (400-700 nm) that phytoplankton can use for photosynthesis
Light is required for photosynthesis
It penetrates only upper 200 meters (euphotic zone)
Its intensity decreases with depth
Affects vertical distribution of phytoplankton
Limiting factor: In deep water and winter months

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors…
2. Nutrient Availability


Macronutrients:

Nitrogen (N) - essential for proteins and nucleic acids
Phosphorus (P) - crucial for ATP and DNA production
Silica (Si) - needed by diatoms for cell walls
Carbon (C) - primary building block of photosynthesis


Micronutrients/Trace Elements:

Iron (Fe) - critical enzyme co-factor (often limiting)
Manganese (Mn) - photosynthesis component
Zinc (Zn) - enzyme co-factor (often limiting)
Copper (Cu) - respiration and photosynthesis component



Nutrient limitation: Occurs when essential nutrients become depleted
Some phytoplankton can fix nitrogen and can grow in areas where nitrate concentrations are low.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors…
3. Temperature

Affects metabolic rates and enzyme activity in phytoplankton
Optimal range: 15-30°C for most species, in tropics, optimal temperature is between 25-30°C
Seasonal effects: Growth varies with seasonal temperature changes
Geographic pattern: Higher productivity in temperate zones seasonally, but lower in tropical areas

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors…
4. Salinity

Determines osmotic balance for phytoplankton cells
Influences nutrient availability and uptake
Affects species composition (freshwater vs. marine species)

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors…
5. Water Turbulence and Mixing

Vertical mixing: Brings nutrient-rich deeper water to surface
Prevents sinking: Keeps phytoplankton in euphotic zone
Optimal conditions: Moderate mixing

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors…
6. Grazing Pressure

Predation by zooplankton: Controls phytoplankton biomass
Top-down control: High grazing = lower phytoplankton abundance
Species selectivity: Zooplankton prefer certain phytoplankton types

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Nutrient Limitation and the “Redfield Ratio”

Redfield Ratio: C:N:P = 106:16:1
Represents the average elemental composition of phytoplankton biomass
Used to predict which nutrient will limit growth

Application:

If actual N:P ratio < 16:1 → Nitrogen limited
If actual N:P ratio > 16:1 → Phosphorus limited
Helps predict ecosystem productivity

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phytoplankton Growth Phases: Overview

Phytoplankton populations follow a characteristic growth pattern with four distinct phases
Each phase represents different population dynamics
Understanding these phases is crucial for predicting bloom events and managing aquatic ecosystems

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phase 1: Lag Phase (Adaptation)


Characteristics:

Phytoplankton acclimate to new environment
Low growth rate
Enzyme and nutrient uptake mechanisms are activated
Cells preparing for rapid growth

Duration:

Variable, typically days to weeks
Depends on environmental conditions


What is happening:

Cells synthesize enzymes
Photosynthetic machinery activated
Nutrient transport systems established
Building internal reserves

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phase 2: Exponential (Log) Growth Phase


Characteristics:

Rapid population growth under optimal conditions
Resources are abundant
Biomass doubles at regular intervals
Maximum growth rate achieved

Duration:

Weeks to months
Continues while resources available


What is happening:

Cells dividing at maximum rate
Nutrients abundant
Light optimal
Waste products not yet limiting
This is when algal blooms occur

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phase 3: Stationary Phase


Characteristics:

Growth rate equals death rate
Nutrient depletion begins
Waste accumulation occurs
Biomass plateaus at carrying capacity

Duration:

Variable, typically weeks
Reflects resource availability


What is happening:

Nutrient availability declining
Competition for resources intensifying
Toxin accumulation possible
Population stabilizes at maximum sustainable level

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phase 4: Death (Decline) Phase


Characteristics:

Nutrients are exhausted
Toxic metabolites accumulate
Death rate exceeds birth rate
Population crashes

Duration:

Days to weeks
Depends on toxin levels


What is happening:

Essential nutrients depleted
Waste products toxic to cells
Cell lysis and death accelerates
Biomass rapidly decreases
Dead cells sink (detritus)

Factors Triggering Phase Transitions
Lag → Exponential:

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology

Factors Triggering Phase Transitions
Exponential → Stationary:

Waste metabolites accumulate
Light intensity decreases

Stationary → Death:

Complete nutrient depletion
Toxic compound buildup
pH changes
Oxygen depletion in enclosed systems

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Biogeographic Distribution of Phytoplankton
Spatial Patterns:

Coastal waters (Neritic): HIGH productivity

Nutrient input from rivers and upwelling
Better light penetration

Open ocean (Oceanic): VARIABLE productivity

Often nutrient-poor (oligotrophic)
“Oceanic deserts” in tropical regions
High productivity in upwelling zones

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Biogeographic Distribution of Phytoplankton
Temporal Patterns:

Seasonal cycles: Spring and fall blooms in temperate zones
Monsoon-driven cycles: In tropical regions with seasonal rains
Daily vertical migration: Some species move up/down
Interannual variability: Related to climate patterns (El Niño, etc.)

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Indicator Species and Ecosystem Health

Phytoplankton as bioindicators:

Changes in species composition = ecosystem changes
Diversity patterns reflect environmental stress
Sensitive to pollution and nutrient enrichment

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Primary Production:


Phytoplankton are primary producers, meaning they convert sunlight, carbon dioxide, and nutrients into organic matter through photosynthesis
They form the base of the marine food web, providing energy and nutrients to higher trophic levels.
Phytoplankton are the foundation of all aquatic food web

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton
2.Carbon Sequestration:

Phytoplankton absorb CO₂ from the atmosphere during photosynthesis and incorporate it into their biomass.
Through photosynthesis, phytoplankton consume carbon dioxide on a scale equivalent to forests and other land plants.
Some of this carbon is carried to the deep ocean when phytoplankton die – effectively removing carbon from the atmosphere and contributing to the regulation of climate.
Some is transferred to different layers of the ocean as phytoplankton are eaten by other creatures, which themselves reproduce, generate waste, and die.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton
2.Carbon Sequestration…:

Worldwide, this biological carbon pump transfers about 10 gigatonnes of carbon from the atmosphere to the deep ocean each year.
Even a small changes in the growth of phytoplankton may affect atmospheric carbon dioxide concentrations, which would feed back to global surface temperatures.
How ????

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Carbon flux in the ocean

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
section: Understanding Carbon Flux: The Biological Carbon Pump
text:
Understanding Carbon Flux: The Biological Carbon Pump
What is the Biological Carbon Pump?

Mechanism by which carbon dioxide (CO₂) is converted into organic matter by phytoplankton and transported to the deep ocean
Acts as a major regulator of atmospheric CO₂ and global climate

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Understanding Carbon Flux…
The Process (Four key steps):

Photosynthetic Fixation (Surface)

Phytoplankton fix atmospheric CO₂ into organic compounds (glucose, proteins, lipids)
Occurs in euphotic zone (0-200 m) where light is available

Food Web Transfer (Surface to Mid-Water)

Zooplankton consume phytoplankton
Fish consume zooplankton
Energy and carbon transfer up the food chain
~90% of energy lost at each trophic level

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Understanding Carbon Flux…

Particle Sinking (Active Transport)

Dead phytoplankton cells form aggregates (“marine snow”)
Dead zooplankton and fecal pellets sink
Particles fall through water column (1-100 m/day)
Passive transport of carbon to depth

Remineralization (Deep Ocean)

Bacteria decompose sinking organic matter
Some carbon sequestered in deep ocean (1000+ years residence time)
Some carbon recycled back to surface via upwelling

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Understanding Carbon Flux…
Global Carbon Flux Numbers:

~10 gigatonnes of carbon/year transferred from atmosphere to deep ocean
~50% of Earth’s primary production is from phytoplankton
~0.5-2 gigatonnes/year permanently sequestered in deep ocean
Equivalent to all carbon emissions from fossil fuels and deforestation combined!

Why This Matters for Climate:

Reduces atmospheric CO₂ by ~25-50% compared to no biological pump
Small changes in phytoplankton productivity = major climate impacts
Ocean warming/acidification affects phytoplankton → reduced CO₂ removal → positive feedback loop

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Food Source:


Phytoplankton are consumed by a wide range of marine organisms, including zooplankton, small fish, shellfish, and whales.
They feed all aquatic organisms from microscopic, animal-like zooplankton to multi-ton whales.
Some small fish and invertebrates they also graze on the phytoplankton, and then those smaller animals are eaten by bigger ones.
They are essential food sources for many marine species, forming the foundation of marine food webs.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Nitrogen fixation


Nitrogen fixation is the biological process by which certain microorganisms, primarily bacteria and cyanobacteria, convert atmospheric nitrogen (N2) into ammonia (NH3) or other nitrogen compounds that can be used by plants and other organisms.
They contribute significant amounts of fixed nitrogen to marine ecosystems.
This input of fixed nitrogen can alleviate nitrogen limitation in phytoplankton communities, promoting their growth and productivity.
Example of nitrogen fixers are such as Trichodesmium and Richelia species

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Nutrient Cycling:


Phytoplankton contribute to nutrient cycling in marine ecosystems by recycling nutrients such as nitrogen, phosphorus, and iron.
They play a vital role in maintaining nutrient balance and productivity in marine environments.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Indicator of Ecosystem Health:


Changes in phytoplankton populations can serve as indicators of environmental changes and ecosystem health.
Monitoring phytoplankton abundance, species composition, and distribution can provide insights into water quality, nutrient levels, and ecosystem dynamics.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Climate Regulation:


Phytoplankton influence climate by reducing atmospheric CO2 levels during photosynthesis process

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Oxygen Production:


Phytoplankton produce a significant portion of oxygen on the Earth through photosynthesis, contributing more than 50% of the oxygen in the Earth’s atmosphere.
They play a major role in maintaining global oxygen levels.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of Phytoplankton

Biotechnological Applications:


Some species of phytoplankton are used in biotechnological applications, such as producing biofuels, pharmaceuticals, and nutritional supplements.
For example, certain types of microalgae are cultivated for their high lipid content, which can be converted into biodiesel.
Microalgae with the highest lipid content, ranging from 10 to 67%, belong to Chlorella, Dunaliella, and Scenedesmus species
A Spirulina species – a blue-green algae, holds immense potential in the field of biotechnology.
Its ability to produce valuable secondary metabolites like nutraceuticals and pharmaceuticals makes it a promising source for drug development
They are effective in managing allergies, cancer, and other diseases

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of phytoplankton
10. Aquaculture

Phytoplankton are a key food item in both aquaculture and mariculture.
Both utilize phytoplankton as food for the animals being farmed.
In mariculture, the phytoplankton is naturally occurring and is introduced into enclosures with the normal circulation of seawater.
In aquaculture, phytoplankton must be introduced directly.
The plankton can either be collected from a body of water or cultured.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of phytoplankton
11. Economic Importance of Phytoplankton

Phytoplanktons have several uses and their mass cultivation can also be a major source of income.
They are mostly used as food supplements and play a vital role in both animal and human nutrition.
Phytoplanktons are also used in the manufacture of drugs.
Also, they have proven to be suitable for synthesizing vaccines.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of phytoplankton
12. Negative importance of phytoplankton

Some phytoplankton species can be the source of death or disease to other aquatic organisms.
Certain species of phytoplankton produce powerful biotoxins – leading to the development of red tides, or harmful algal blooms (HABs).
These toxic blooms can kill marine life and people who eat contaminated seafood.
Phytoplankton may cause mass mortality in other ways.
In the aftermath of a massive bloom, dead phytoplankton sink to the ocean or lake floor.
Then, decomposition occurs aided by bacteria.
This process lead to depletion of oxygen in the water, causing to suffocation of animals.
This may result to a massive death of aquatic organisms.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Terms in phytoplankton
Hormogonia – Motile trichomes of cells (used for dispersal) that are often gas-vacuolated and devoid of heterocysts, with a distinct cell shape and size compared to vegetative cells.





Oscillatoria species






Hormogonia of oscillatoria formed during vegetative reproduction

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Terms in phytoplankton


Trichome is a chain of connected cells in filamentous cyanobacteria, functioning as a single physiological unit.

Trichomes are simply linear or coiled arrangements of cells within a shared sheath.
It includes the cell wall but not the sheath.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Features of Trichomes



Composed of a cell.
This can be uniform (e.g., Oscillatoria) or differentiated into specialized cells:

Vegetative cells: Perform photosynthesis.
Heterocysts: Fix nitrogen (thick-walled, pale).
Akinetes: Dormant cell (spores) (survival under stress).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Terms in phytoplankton


Filament refers to a multicellular, thread-like structure composed of a linear chain of cells (trichome) enclosed within a protective mucilaginous sheath.

Filamentous cyanobacteria are among the most ecologically significant, as they often dominate blooms and contribute to nitrogen fixation.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Terms in phytoplankton


Heterocyst is a specialized, nitrogen-fixing cell found in filamentous cyanobacteria (e.g., Anabaena, Nostoc).

These cells are critical for converting atmospheric nitrogen (\(N_2\)) into ammonia (\(NH_3\)), a process called nitrogen fixation, which is essential for nutrient cycling in aquatic and terrestrial ecosystems.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
section: Structural and Functional Features of Heterocysts
text:
Structural and Functional Features of Heterocysts

Thickened cell wall: Contains glycolipids to block oxygen (\(O_2\)) which inhibits nitrogenase, the enzyme for \(N_2\) fixation).
Pale appearance: Lacks phycobiliproteins (no photosynthesis) but retains chlorophyll-a.
Triggered by nitrogen limitation (e.g., low \(NO_3^-\) or \(NH_4^+\)).
No Photosystem II (PS-II): Prevents \(O_2\) production.
Uses Photosystem I (PS-I): Generates ATP for nitrogenase.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological importance of Heterocysts
Nitrogen Supply:

It provides fixed nitrogen (\(NH_3\)) to cyanobacteria and surrounding organisms.

Bloom formation:

It enables cyanobacteria (e.g., Anabaena) to dominate in nitrogen-poor waters.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Why are heterocysts oxygen-sensitive?

Nitrogenase, the enzyme that catalyzes nitrogen fixation (\(N_2 \rightarrow NH_3\)), is irreversibly inactivated/damaged by \(O_2\).
Therefore, heterocysts maintain an anaerobic environment by:

Blocking \(O_2\) diffusion (thickened wall).
Respiration: Uses \(O_2\) for ATP synthesis.
No PS-II: Prevents \(O_2\) generation.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Terms in phytoplankton

Akinete: Is a thick-walled, dormant spore formed by some filamentous cyanobacteria under unfavorable conditions (e.g., nutrient limitation, cold, or desiccation).
Akinetes allow survival in harsh environments and regenerate when conditions improve.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Structure of Akinetes

Enlarged cells: Larger than vegetative cells, with thickened cell walls.
Storage reserves: Accumulate glycogen, cyanophycin (nitrogen reserve), and lipids.
Pigments: Retain chlorophyll-a but reduce photosynthetic activity.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Formation of akinetes

Akinetes are formed if the conditions are unfavorable when there is:


Nutrient depletion (especially phosphorus or nitrogen).
Temperature shifts (cold or freezing).
Desiccation (prolonged dryness).
Light limitation.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Germination of akinetes

When favorable conditions return:


Wall breaks down.
Cell divides to form a new filament.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Vegetative cells

Vegetative cells are the primary photosynthetic cells in cyanobacteria, responsible for growth and energy production.
They contain chlorophyll-a and phycobiliproteins for photosynthesis and are typically thin-walled and actively dividing under favorable conditions.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Types of vegetative cell structures



Unicellular – Individual cells exist independently, often spherical or rod-shaped. Example: Chroococcus.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Types of vegetative cell structures



Colonial – Groups of cells are held together by a mucilaginous sheath but remain loosely associated. Example: Microcystis.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Types of vegetative cell structures



Pseudofilamentous – Cells form a chain-like arrangement but lack true cellular connections, appearing filamentous. Example: Merismopedia.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Types of vegetative cell structures



Filamentous – Cells form long, connected chains called trichomes, sharing a common sheath. Example: Oscillatoria.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Types of vegetative cell structures



False Branching – Filaments appear branched but lack continuous cytoplasmic connections. Example: Scytonema.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Types of vegetative cell structures



True Branching – Filaments divide in different directions with continuous cytoplasmic connections. Example: Stigonema.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Classification of phytoplankton

Phytoplankton can be classified into three major groups which include the Cyanobacteria (Cyanophyta), Diatoms (Bacillariophyta), and Dinoflagellates (Dinophyta).
Some other groups includes Coccolithophores (Haptophyta), Green Algae (Chlorophyta), Cryptophytes (Cryptophyta).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Cyanobacteria (Blue-green Algae)

Cyanobacteria are group of autotrophic, Gram-negative bacteria that obtain biological energy via oxygenic photosynthesis.
The name cyanobacteria originates from the Greek word kyanós, referring to their characteristic bluish-green (cyan) color.
This color forms the basis of their informal name, “blue-green algae,” although as prokaryotes, they are not scientifically classified as true algae.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Cyanobacteria (Blue-green algae)

They utilize photosynthetic pigments—including chlorophyll-a, carotenoids, and phycobilins (phycocyanin and phycoerythrin)—to harvest light energy.
Unlike most other bacteria, they possess an extensive system of internal membranes called thylakoids – flattened sacs, from which photosynthesis is performed.
They are the only prokaryotes capable of oxygenic photosynthesis, a process that significantly contributed to the oxygenation of Earth’s early atmosphere.
Many species produce potent cyanotoxins (e.g., microcystins, anatoxins) that pose significant risks to water quality, livestock, and human health.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Cyanobacteria Orders
The Phylum Cyanobacteria is traditionally divided into several orders based on their morphological complexity:

Chroococcales: Unicellular or colonial forms; no filaments.
Oscillatoriales: Simple filamentous forms; no specialized cells (heterocysts/akinetes).
Nostocales: Filamentous forms with specialized cells (heterocysts and akinetes); false branching.
Stigonematales: Most complex; true branching and multiseriate filaments.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Cyanobacteria Orders…






Evolutionary Trend


The classification reflects an evolutionary progression from simple single cells to complex, multicellular-like structures with functional division of labor.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Order Chroococcales

The Chroococcales is an order of cyanobacteria known for including significant bloom-forming species like Microcystis aeruginosa.
They are primarily characterized by unicellular or colonial (non-filamentous) growth forms.
As one of the most ancient lineages, they are globally distributed across freshwater, brackish, and marine environments.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Chroococcales


Morphology

They are unicellular (e.g., Synechococcus) or colonial (e.g., Microcystis).
Cells are typically spherical, oval, or rod-shaped.
Colony formations include:

Irregular clusters (Microcystis).
Cubical packets (Merismopedia).
Embedded in a gelatinous matrix (Gloeocapsa).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Chroococcales…
Cell Structure

Prokaryotic organization: Lack a membrane-bound nucleus and organelles.
Gram-negative cell wall: Composed of a peptidoglycan layer and an outer membrane.
Mucilaginous sheath: A protective polysaccharide layer that aids in buoyancy and protection against desiccation.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Chroococcales…
Reproduction

Reproduction by binary fission (asexual reproduction).
Colony fragmentation: Large colonies break into smaller aggregates to establish new populations.
Lack specialized reproductive cells (no akinetes or heterocysts).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Chroococcales…
Pigments & photosynthesis

They utilize chlorophyll-a alongside accessory phycobiliproteins (phycocyanin and phycoerythrin) to capture a broad spectrum of light.
They perform oxygenic photosynthesis within thylakoid membranes.
Unlike filamentous orders, they lack heterocysts; therefore, they cannot fix atmospheric nitrogen and rely on dissolved inorganic nitrogen (e.g., \(NO_3^-\), \(NH_4^+\)).
Many species (e.g., Microcystis) possess gas vacuoles to regulate their position in the water column for optimal light harvesting.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Taxonomic classification of Chroococcales

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Common Genera & Species

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological & economic importance of Chroococcales
Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs):

Microcystis and Chroococcus can form dense surface scums that deplete oxygen and block light.
Microcystis produces microcystins, potent hepatotoxins (liver toxins) that threaten drinking water and aquatic life.

Primary Production:

Genera like Synechococcus are major contributors to global carbon fixation, especially in oligotrophic (nutrient-poor) oceans.

Bioindicators:

High abundance of Microcystis is often used as a bioindicator of eutrophication (excessive nutrient enrichment).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Order Oscillatoriales


The Oscillatoriales is an order of filamentous cyanobacteria distinguished by their structural simplicity:

Unbranched trichomes: Simple, linear chains of cells.
No specialized cells: They lack heterocysts and akinetes, relying on dissolved nitrogen.
Reproduction: Primarily through hormogonia (short motile segments) or filament fragmentation.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Oscillatoriales


Morphology & structure

Unbranched trichomes: Simple linear chains of cells.
No specialized cells: Lack heterocysts and akinetes.
Cell Shape: Discoid or cylindrical with distinct cross-walls.
Sheath: Usually thin or absent, allowing for movement.

Cellular Composition

Pigments: Chlorophyll-a, phycocyanin, and phycoerythrin.
Prokaryotic: No membrane-bound organelles.


Motility & reproduction

Gliding movement: Active movement via mucilage secretion and contractile fibrils.
Fragmentation: Trichomes break into smaller pieces (hormogonia) to grow.
Hormogonia: Specialized short, motile segments used for dispersal.

Ecological niche

Often found in eutrophic (nutrient-rich) waters.
Can form dense benthic mats or surface blooms.








Key identification


The absence of heterocysts and akinetes in a filamentous cyanobacterium is a primary diagnostic feature of the Order Oscillatoriales, distinguishing them from the more complex Nostocales.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Taxonomic classification

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Common genera & species

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological & Economic Importance
Harmful Algal Blooms (HABs):

Lyngbya produces lyngbyatoxin, a potent skin irritant causing “swimmers’ itch.”
Oscillatoria can dominate eutrophic waters, leading to oxygen depletion and foul odors.

Biofilms & Mats:

Phormidium forms dense benthic mats in freshwater, stabilizing sediments but potentially harboring toxins.

Commercial Uses:

Arthrospira (Spirulina) is globally cultivated as a nutrient-rich “superfood” due to its high protein and vitamin content.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Order Nostocales

The Nostocales are a morphologically diverse order of filamentous cyanobacteria characterized by the presence of specialized nitrogen-fixing cells (heterocysts) and often dormant spores (akinetes).
They play crucial ecological roles in nitrogen cycling and form symbiotic relationships with plants and fungi.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Nostocales
Morphology

Consists of a filamentous structure composed of trichomes (cell chains) enclosed in a mucilaginous sheath.
They have a specialized cells composed of:

Heterocysts: Thick-walled, pale cells for nitrogen fixation.
Akinetes: Enlarged, spore-like cells for survival under harsh environment.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Common growth forms include:

Linear filaments (Anabaena): Simple, straight chains of cells that often dominate open-water phytoplankton communities.
Coiled or Globose filaments (Nostoc): Filaments that twist or aggregate into gelatinous colonies, providing protection against grazing.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Nostocales
Cell Structure

Prokaryotic organization: Lack a membrane-bound nucleus and organelles.
Photosynthetic pigments:

Chlorophyll-a: Primary pigment for photosynthesis.
Phycobiliproteins: Accessory pigments (phycocyanin, allophycocyanin) for light harvesting.
Carotenoids: Provide photoprotection and light absorption.

Cell Wall: Gram-negative structure featuring a distinct peptidoglycan layer and an outer membrane.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Nostocales


Nitrogen Fixation

Heterocysts provide the low-oxygen conditions required for the nitrogenase enzyme to function.
Heterocyst positioning: – where these cells are located along the filament (trichome) can vary by species and environmental conditions and used to identify different genera:

Terminal: Located at the ends of filaments (e.g., Cylindrospermum).
Intercalary: Located between vegetative cells (e.g., Anabaena).
Suppression: Formation is often inhibited in nitrogen-rich environments.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Nostocales
Reproduction & dispersal

Binary fission: Standard cell division for filament elongation.
Fragmentation: Mechanical breaking of filaments into smaller colonies.
Hormogonia: Specialized, short, motile segments that detach for dispersal.
Akinetes: Thick-walled resting spores that ensure survival during environmental stress.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Taxonomic classification of Nostocales

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Common genera & species

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Order Stigonematales

The Stigonematales represent the most morphologically complex cyanobacteria, characterized by true branching filaments and multiseriate trichomes (filaments with cells arranged in multiple rows).
This order contains species with the highest degree of cellular differentiation among cyanobacteria.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Stigonematales


Morphological complexity

True branching: Both T-type (transverse cell division) and Y-type (longitudinal division) branching
Multiseriate trichomes: Filaments composed of multiple cell rows (unlike other cyanobacteria)
Thick, laminated sheaths: Often pigmented (yellow, brown, or red)
Cell differentiation composed of:

Heterocysts (nitrogen-fixing cells)
Akinetes (resting spores)
Vegetative cells of varying shapes/sizes

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Stigonematales
Cellular structure

Prokaryotic organization but with complex thylakoid arrangements
Pigments composed of:

Chlorophyll-a
Phycobiliproteins (phycocyanin dominant)
Unique carotenoids (e.g., nostoxanthin)

Cell walls: Thickened with additional structural layers

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of Stigonematales
Growth forms & habitat

Mat-forming species
Symbiotic species (with bryophytes)
Commonly found in moist soils, rocks, and thermal springs.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Reproduction in Stigonematales

Hormogonia: Short, motile segments of the trichome that detach for dispersal and colonization.
Akinetes: Thick-walled resting spores that allow survival during periods of desiccation or nutrient stress.
Fragmentation: Mechanical breaking of the complex branched filaments into smaller viable units.
Binary Fission: Occurs in multiple planes, leading to the characteristic true branching and multiseriate (multiple rows) structure.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Taxonomic classification

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Overview of Ocean Division

Ocean Divisions

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Overview of Ocean Division



The ocean environment is divided based on:

Depth (vertical zones)
Distance from shore (horizontal zones)

These divisions help us understand the distribution of plankton and marine organisms
Each zone has unique characteristics affecting life forms





Ocean Divisions

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Vertical Zones: Pelagic Division

Pelagic zone: The entire water column (open water)
Divided into distinct depth zones based on light penetration and pressure

Major Pelagic Zones:

Epipelagic (Euphotic) – 0-200 m
Mesopelagic (Twilight) – 200-1000 m
Bathypelagic (Midnight) – 1000-6000 m
Hadopelagic (Abyssal) – >6000 m

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Epipelagic Zone (Sunlight Zone)

Depth: 0-200 meters
Characteristics:

Receives sunlight
Warmest zone
Supports photosynthesis
Highest biodiversity
Dissolved oxygen abundant

Plankton: Rich in phytoplankton and zooplankton
Importance: Primary production occurs here

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Mesopelagic Zone (Twilight Zone)

Depth: 200-1000 meters
Characteristics:

Dim light (partial darkness)
Twilight conditions (not enough for photosynthesis)
Rapid temperature decrease
Lower photosynthesis

Plankton: Specialized zooplankton adapted to low light
Organisms: Bioluminescent species common

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Bathypelagic Zone (Midnight Zone)

Depth: 1000-6000 meters
Characteristics:

Complete darkness
Cold temperatures (2-4°C)
High pressure
Low oxygen in some regions

Plankton: Sparse plankton communities
Energy source: Marine snow (falling organic matter)

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Hadopelagic Zone (Abyssal Zone)

Depth: >6000 meters
Characteristics:

Extreme darkness
Crushing pressure
Near-freezing temperatures
Minimal life

Plankton: Extremely specialized and rare
Organisms: Found near hydrothermal vents

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Horizontal Zones: Neritic vs. Oceanic
Neritic Province:

Shallow water above the continental shelf
Extends 200+ km from shore
High nutrient availability
High productivity
Supports rich plankton communities

Oceanic Province:

Deep water beyond the continental shelf
Low nutrient availability (except upwelling zones)
Lower productivity in many regions
Unique specialized plankton

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Benthic Division

Benthic zone: The seafloor and organisms living on/in it
Divided by depth:

Supralittoral – Above high tide
Littoral – Intertidal zone
Sublittoral – Continental shelf (< 200 m)
Abyssal – Deep ocean floor

Meroplankton larvae settle in benthic zones

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Key Factors Affecting Ocean Zones



Factor
Impact




Light
Enables photosynthesis; affects animal behavior


Temperature
Controls metabolic rates and organism distribution


Pressure
Affects gas solubility and organism physiology


Dissolved Oxygen
Essential for respiration; varies by depth


Nutrients
Crucial for primary production; limits growth


Salinity
Affects osmotic balance in organisms

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Plankton Distribution by Zone


Epipelagic:

Rich plankton communities
Maximum phytoplankton
Diverse zooplankton
Larval forms

Mesopelagic:

Deep-sea copepods
Larval fishes
Myctophids (lanternfish)


Bathypelagic:

Specialized zooplankton
Detritus feeders
Bioluminescent species

Hadopelagic:

Hadal amphipods
Sparse copepods
Specialized bacteria

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of Ocean Divisions

Scientific Understanding:

Predicting organism distribution
Understanding adaptation strategies
Tracking nutrient cycling

Conservation:

Managing marine resources
Understanding climate impacts
Protecting biodiversity

Fisheries:

Locating commercial species
Sustainable harvest practices

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Summary

Ocean divisions provide a framework for understanding marine ecosystems
Vertical divisions (epipelagic, mesopelagic, bathypelagic, hadopelagic) depend on depth and light
Horizontal divisions (neritic, oceanic) depend on distance from shore
Each zone has unique physical conditions
Plankton distributions reflect these environmental gradients

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: This course provides fundamentals of taxonomy, ecology, and importance of plankton in aquatic ecosystems. Students will learn about the diversity of planktonic organisms, their roles in food webs, and methods for studying plankton communities.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: This course provides fundamentals of taxonomy, ecology, and importance of plankton in aquatic ecosystems. Students will learn about the diversity of planktonic organisms, their roles in food webs, and methods for studying plankton communities.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Course Objectives
To introduce students to the systematics of both phytoplankton and zooplankton; plankton sampling methods and determination of productivity; plankton ecology and productivity

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Course Synopsis/Modules

Module 1: Course Introduction

Introduction to plankton and their importance in aquatic ecosystems.
Classification of Plankton
Division of the aquatic environment



Module 2: Plankton Ecology

Systematic and biology of phytoplankton and Zooplankton (holoplankton and meroplankton).
Adaptation to the environment:

Structural adaptations to floatation, aids of floatation
Cyclomorphosis, colour and transparency, bioluminescence




Module 3: Plankton Productivity

Primary production in the aquatic environment.
Secondary production and the food webs.
Bacterial production and the concept of the microbial loop.
Factors controlling the distribution and productivity.
Nutrient regeneration and the role of zooplankton in nutrient cycling.
Trophodynamic relationships.
Feeding processes: rate of filtering, prey selection.
Zooplankton variations and the concept of indicator species.
Zoogeography of the holoplankton.
Vertical migration: seasonal, ontogenic and diel-vertical migration.
Spatial and seasonal changes of phytoplankton and zooplankton.



Module 4: Harmful Microalgae

Harmful algal blooms (HABs): causes, effects, and management strategies.
Types of HABs



Module 5: Sampling Methods and Laboratory Analysis

Methods of measurement of biomass and primary production, bacterial production, and nitrogen fixation in the water column.
Quantitative phytoplankton and zooplankton sampling; methods of collecting, fixing and preserving.
Laboratory analyses of planktons.

title: About
text: About this site

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Plankton



The word plankton comes from a Greek word planktos, meaning drifter or wanderer.
Aquatic organisms that are incapable of swimming or moving on their own.
Their movement in water is aided by water movement such as tides, currents, waves
Most plankton are microscopic, often less than one inch in length.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Plankton…



They also include larger species like some crustaceans and jellyfish.
Some organisms are planktonic for their entire life cycle.
Others are planktonic only when they are young, but they eventually grow large enough to swim against the currents.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Classification of Plankton

Plankton can be classified in several ways based on their;

Size
Life cycle
Trophic level

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Classification based on size

Based on their size, plankton can be classified as

Megaplankton – > 20 cm eg. Jellyfish
Macroplankton – 2-20 cm eg. Pteropods, krill
Mesoplankton – 0.2 mm-2 cm eg. copepods, Foraminifera
Microplankton – 20-200 \({\mu}m\) eg. Ciliates, coccolithophores
Nanoplankton – 2-20 \({\mu}m\) eg. Diatoms, dinoflagellates
Picoplankton – 0.2-2 \({\mu}m\) eg. smaller eukaryotic protists, bacteria
Femtoplankton – < 0.2 \({\mu}m\) eg. marine viruses

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Classification based on life cycle

Depending on mode of life cycle, plankton can be classified as:

Holoplankton or
Meroplankton

Holoplankton are organisms that are planktonic for their entire life cycle.
Holoplankton lives in pelagic zone in aquatic environment

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Classification based on life cycle

Pelagic organisms are divided into two groups; plankton and nekton.
Nektons are capable of swimming against water current and include most species of sharks, rays, fishes, seals, sea lions, and whales.
Examples of holoplankton are some diatoms, radiolarians, some dinoflagellates, foraminifera, amphipods, krill, copepods, and salps, as well as some gastropod mollusk species.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Classification based on life cycle

Meroplankton are aquatic organisms which have both planktonic and benthic stages in their life cycles.
Many of the meroplankton consists of larval stages of larger organism (Stübner et al., 2016)
After a period of time in the plankton, many meroplankton changes to the nekton or adopt a benthic (often sessile) lifestyle on the seafloor.
Many benthic community are planktonic during their larval stages (Ershova et al., 2019)

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Classification based on life cycle

The planktonic larval stage is particularly crucial to many benthic invertebrate in order to disperse their young.
Depending on particular species and environmental conditions, larval or juvenile-stage meroplankton may remain in the pelagic zone for duration ranging from an hour to months

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Classification based on Trophic level

Plankton can also be divided based on the trophic level
This is the most basic category that divide plankton into two groups;

Phytoplankton – plants and
Zooplankton – animals.

Zooplankton and other small marine organisms feed on phytoplankton and they become food for fish, crustaceans, and other larger species.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Distribution of plankton

Plankton inhabits oceans, seas, lakes and ponds.
Their abundance varies both horizontally, vertically and seasonally.
This is due to availability of light.
Another factor causing plankton variation is nutrient availability.
Although large areas of tropical and sub-tropical oceans have abundant light, but they experience relatively low primary production because they offer limited nutrients such as nitrate, phosphate, iron and silicate.
In such regions, primary production usually occurs at greater depth, but at a reduced level due to limited nutrient level.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Aquatic environment divisions

Aquatic ecosystems can be sub-divided into:

Vertical division
Horizontal division

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Vertical division

This has two sub-groups:

Benthic

Entire bottom, or the substratum and its attached and crawling organisms on the substratum.

Pelagic

Entire water body excluding the benthic system; It deals with floating and swimming organisms.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Horizontal division

Two major parts:

Neritic province: – Shallow water above the continental shelf.
Oceanic: – Deep water beyond the continental shelf.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Aquatic environment divisions

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
References




Ershova, E., Descoteaux, R., Wangensteen, O., Iken, K., Hopcroft, R., Smoot, C., Grebmeier, J.M., Bluhm, B.A., 2019. Diversity and distribution of meroplanktonic larvae in the pacific arctic and connectivity with adult benthic invertebrate communities. Frontiers in Marine Science 6, 490.


Stübner, E., Søreide, J., Reigstad, M., Marquardt, M., Blachowiak-Samolyk, K., 2016. Year-round meroplankton dynamics in high-arctic svalbard. Journal of Plankton Research 38, 522–536.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Terminologies used in Plankton Ecology
Plankton systematics

The study of the classification and taxonomy of planktonic organisms.
It involves identifying and categorizing different species of plankton based on their morphological, physiological, ecological, and genetic characteristics of planktonic organisms.
This field helps us understand the diversity and evolutionary relationships among planktonic species, as well as their distribution patterns and ecological roles in aquatic ecosystems.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Terminologies…
Plankton ecology

Plankton ecology is the study of planktonic organisms and their interactions with each other and their environment within aquatic ecosystems.
It includes the distribution, abundance, diversity, behavior, physiology, and ecological roles of plankton.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Terminologies…
Plankton productivity

Plankton productivity refers to the rate at which planktonic organisms produce organic matter through photosynthesis and other metabolic processes.
It is a critical component of aquatic ecosystems, as plankton are the primary producers that form the base of the food web and support the entire ecosystem.
Understanding plankton productivity is essential for understanding the dynamics of aquatic ecosystems and the impacts of human activities on these ecosystems.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
What is Phytoplankton?

Phytoplankton is derived from the Greek words:

Phyto – (plant)
Plankton — (wanderer or drifter)

Phytoplankton are microscopic aquatic plants which do not have ability to swim
They are free-floating aquatic organisms that depend entirely on water movement such as surface currents for their movement.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phytoplankton…

They thrive in euphotic zone; the upper water column of the world’s oceans, lakes, rivers, ponds.
The euphotic zone receives energy from the sun that allows phytoplankton to photosynthesize – a chemical reaction that converts light energy into chemical energy (Peter et al., 2018).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Phytoplankton…

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
What is Phytoplankton Ecology?

Definition: Study of phytoplankton organisms and their interactions with each other and their aquatic environment
Key focus areas:

Distribution and abundance patterns
Physiological responses to environmental factors
Population dynamics and growth rates
Ecological roles in food webs and nutrient cycling
Community structure and biodiversity

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
section: Photosynthesis: The Heart of Phytoplankton Ecology
text:
Photosynthesis: The Heart of Phytoplankton Ecology



Phytoplankton contain Chlorophyll-a (Chl-a) – a green pigment responsible for photosynthesis
Chl-a captures light energy and converts it into chemical energy (glucose)
This is the foundation of all aquatic food webs


Key products:

Organic matter (glucose) - food for consumers
Oxygen (O₂) - byproduct for respiration
Accounts for ~50% of Earth’s oxygen production

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Photosynthesis…
The Photosynthetic Equation:
\[6CO_2 + 6H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Sunlight + Chlorophyll}} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2\]

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Primary Productivity

Definition: Rate at which phytoplankton produce organic matter through photosynthesis
Two types:

Gross Primary Productivity (GPP): Total organic matter produced
Net Primary Productivity (NPP): Organic matter remaining after phytoplankton respiration

Global significance: Phytoplankton account for ~50% of total organic matter production on Earth

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors Controlling Phytoplankton Growth
1. Light (Photosynthetically Active Radiation - PAR)

PAR is the portion of sunlight (400-700 nm) that phytoplankton can use for photosynthesis
Light is required for photosynthesis
It penetrates only upper 200 meters (euphotic zone)
Its intensity decreases with depth
Affects vertical distribution of phytoplankton
Limiting factor: In deep water and winter months

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors…
2. Nutrient Availability


Macronutrients:

Nitrogen (N) - essential for proteins and nucleic acids
Phosphorus (P) - crucial for ATP and DNA production
Silica (Si) - needed by diatoms for cell walls
Carbon (C) - primary building block of photosynthesis


Micronutrients/Trace Elements:

Iron (Fe) - critical enzyme co-factor (often limiting)
Manganese (Mn) - photosynthesis component
Zinc (Zn) - enzyme co-factor (often limiting)
Copper (Cu) - respiration and photosynthesis component



Nutrient limitation: Occurs when essential nutrients become depleted
Some phytoplankton can fix nitrogen and can grow in areas where nitrate concentrations are low.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors…
3. Temperature

Affects metabolic rates and enzyme activity in phytoplankton
Optimal range: 15-30°C for most species, in tropics, optimal temperature is between 25-30°C
Seasonal effects: Growth varies with seasonal temperature changes
Geographic pattern: Higher productivity in temperate zones seasonally, but lower in tropical areas

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors…
4. Salinity

Determines osmotic balance for phytoplankton cells
Influences nutrient availability and uptake
Affects species composition (freshwater vs. marine species)

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors…
5. Water Turbulence and Mixing

Vertical mixing: Brings nutrient-rich deeper water to surface
Prevents sinking: Keeps phytoplankton in euphotic zone
Optimal conditions: Moderate mixing

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Factors…
6. Grazing Pressure

Predation by zooplankton: Controls phytoplankton biomass
Top-down control: High grazing = lower phytoplankton abundance
Species selectivity: Zooplankton prefer certain phytoplankton types

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Nutrient Limitation and the “Redfield Ratio”

Redfield Ratio: C:N:P = 106:16:1
Represents the average elemental composition of phytoplankton biomass
Used to predict which nutrient will limit growth

Application:

If actual N:P ratio < 16:1 → Nitrogen limited
If actual N:P ratio > 16:1 → Phosphorus limited
Helps predict ecosystem productivity

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phytoplankton Growth Phases: Overview

Phytoplankton populations follow a characteristic growth pattern with four distinct phases
Each phase represents different population dynamics
Understanding these phases is crucial for predicting bloom events and managing aquatic ecosystems

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phase 1: Lag Phase (Adaptation)


Characteristics:

Phytoplankton acclimate to new environment
Low growth rate
Enzyme and nutrient uptake mechanisms are activated
Cells preparing for rapid growth

Duration:

Variable, typically days to weeks
Depends on environmental conditions


What is happening:

Cells synthesize enzymes
Photosynthetic machinery activated
Nutrient transport systems established
Building internal reserves

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phase 2: Exponential (Log) Growth Phase


Characteristics:

Rapid population growth under optimal conditions
Resources are abundant
Biomass doubles at regular intervals
Maximum growth rate achieved

Duration:

Weeks to months
Continues while resources available


What is happening:

Cells dividing at maximum rate
Nutrients abundant
Light optimal
Waste products not yet limiting
This is when algal blooms occur

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phase 3: Stationary Phase


Characteristics:

Growth rate equals death rate
Nutrient depletion begins
Waste accumulation occurs
Biomass plateaus at carrying capacity

Duration:

Variable, typically weeks
Reflects resource availability


What is happening:

Nutrient availability declining
Competition for resources intensifying
Toxin accumulation possible
Population stabilizes at maximum sustainable level

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phase 4: Death (Decline) Phase


Characteristics:

Nutrients are exhausted
Toxic metabolites accumulate
Death rate exceeds birth rate
Population crashes

Duration:

Days to weeks
Depends on toxin levels


What is happening:

Essential nutrients depleted
Waste products toxic to cells
Cell lysis and death accelerates
Biomass rapidly decreases
Dead cells sink (detritus)

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Primary Production:


Phytoplankton are primary producers, meaning they convert sunlight, carbon dioxide, and nutrients into organic matter through photosynthesis
They form the base of the marine food web, providing energy and nutrients to higher trophic levels.
Phytoplankton are the foundation of all aquatic food web

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton
2.Carbon Sequestration:

Phytoplankton absorb CO₂ from the atmosphere during photosynthesis and incorporate it into their biomass.
Through photosynthesis, phytoplankton consume carbon dioxide on a scale equivalent to forests and other land plants.
Some of this carbon is carried to the deep ocean when phytoplankton die – effectively removing carbon from the atmosphere and contributing to the regulation of climate.
Some is transferred to different layers of the ocean as phytoplankton are eaten by other creatures, which themselves reproduce, generate waste, and die.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton
2.Carbon Sequestration…:

Worldwide, this biological carbon pump transfers about 10 gigatonnes of carbon from the atmosphere to the deep ocean each year.
Even a small changes in the growth of phytoplankton may affect atmospheric carbon dioxide concentrations, which would feed back to global surface temperatures.
How ????

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Carbon flux in the ocean

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
section: Understanding Carbon Flux: The Biological Carbon Pump
text:
Understanding Carbon Flux: The Biological Carbon Pump
What is the Biological Carbon Pump?

Mechanism by which carbon dioxide (CO₂) is converted into organic matter by phytoplankton and transported to the deep ocean
Acts as a major regulator of atmospheric CO₂ and global climate

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Understanding Carbon Flux…
The Process (Four key steps):

Photosynthetic Fixation (Surface)

Phytoplankton fix atmospheric CO₂ into organic compounds (glucose, proteins, lipids)
Occurs in euphotic zone (0-200 m) where light is available

Food Web Transfer (Surface to Mid-Water)

Zooplankton consume phytoplankton
Fish consume zooplankton
Energy and carbon transfer up the food chain
~90% of energy lost at each trophic level

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Understanding Carbon Flux…

Particle Sinking (Active Transport)

Dead phytoplankton cells form aggregates (“marine snow”)
Dead zooplankton and fecal pellets sink
Particles fall through water column (1-100 m/day)
Passive transport of carbon to depth

Remineralization (Deep Ocean)

Bacteria decompose sinking organic matter
Some carbon sequestered in deep ocean (1000+ years residence time)
Some carbon recycled back to surface via upwelling

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Understanding Carbon Flux…
Global Carbon Flux Numbers:

~10 gigatonnes of carbon/year transferred from atmosphere to deep ocean
~50% of Earth’s primary production is from phytoplankton
~0.5-2 gigatonnes/year permanently sequestered in deep ocean
Equivalent to all carbon emissions from fossil fuels and deforestation combined!

Why This Matters for Climate:

Reduces atmospheric CO₂ by ~25-50% compared to no biological pump
Small changes in phytoplankton productivity = major climate impacts
Ocean warming/acidification affects phytoplankton → reduced CO₂ removal → positive feedback loop

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Food Source:


Phytoplankton are consumed by a wide range of marine organisms, including zooplankton, small fish, shellfish, and whales.
They feed all aquatic organisms from microscopic, animal-like zooplankton to multi-ton whales.
Some small fish and invertebrates they also graze on the phytoplankton, and then those smaller animals are eaten by bigger ones.
They are essential food sources for many marine species, forming the foundation of marine food webs.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Nitrogen fixation


Nitrogen fixation is the biological process by which certain microorganisms, primarily bacteria and cyanobacteria, convert atmospheric nitrogen (N2) into ammonia (NH3) or other nitrogen compounds that can be used by plants and other organisms.
They contribute significant amounts of fixed nitrogen to marine ecosystems.
This input of fixed nitrogen can alleviate nitrogen limitation in phytoplankton communities, promoting their growth and productivity.
Example of nitrogen fixers are such as Trichodesmium and Richelia species

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Nutrient Cycling:


Phytoplankton contribute to nutrient cycling in marine ecosystems by recycling nutrients such as nitrogen, phosphorus, and iron.
They play a vital role in maintaining nutrient balance and productivity in marine environments.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Indicator of Ecosystem Health:


Changes in phytoplankton populations can serve as indicators of environmental changes and ecosystem health.
Monitoring phytoplankton abundance, species composition, and distribution can provide insights into water quality, nutrient levels, and ecosystem dynamics.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Climate Regulation:


Phytoplankton influence climate by reducing atmospheric CO2 levels during photosynthesis process

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Ecological Roles of Phytoplankton

Oxygen Production:


Phytoplankton produce a significant portion of oxygen on the Earth through photosynthesis, contributing more than 50% of the oxygen in the Earth’s atmosphere.
They play a major role in maintaining global oxygen levels.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of Phytoplankton

Biotechnological Applications:


Some species of phytoplankton are used in biotechnological applications, such as producing biofuels, pharmaceuticals, and nutritional supplements.
For example, certain types of microalgae are cultivated for their high lipid content, which can be converted into biodiesel.
Microalgae with the highest lipid content, ranging from 10 to 67%, belong to Chlorella, Dunaliella, and Scenedesmus species
A Spirulina species – a blue-green algae, holds immense potential in the field of biotechnology.
Its ability to produce valuable secondary metabolites like nutraceuticals and pharmaceuticals makes it a promising source for drug development
They are effective in managing allergies, cancer, and other diseases

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of phytoplankton
10. Aquaculture

Phytoplankton are a key food item in both aquaculture and mariculture.
Both utilize phytoplankton as food for the animals being farmed.
In mariculture, the phytoplankton is naturally occurring and is introduced into enclosures with the normal circulation of seawater.
In aquaculture, phytoplankton must be introduced directly.
The plankton can either be collected from a body of water or cultured.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of phytoplankton
11. Economic Importance of Phytoplankton

Phytoplanktons have several uses and their mass cultivation can also be a major source of income.
They are mostly used as food supplements and play a vital role in both animal and human nutrition.
Phytoplanktons are also used in the manufacture of drugs.
Also, they have proven to be suitable for synthesizing vaccines.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Phytoplanktons and their uses


PhytoplanktonApplicationChlorella vulgarisFood supplement, animal feedSpirulinaFood supplement and cosmeticsOdontella auritaCosmetics, pharmaceuticals and food for both adults and infantsPhaeodactylum tricornutumFuel production and nutritionPorphyridium cruentumCosmetics, pharmaceuticals and nutrition

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Importance of phytoplankton
12. Negative importance of phytoplankton

Some phytoplankton species can be the source of death or disease to other aquatic organisms.
Certain species of phytoplankton produce powerful biotoxins – leading to the development of red tides, or harmful algal blooms (HABs).
These toxic blooms can kill marine life and people who eat contaminated seafood.
Phytoplankton may cause mass mortality in other ways.
In the aftermath of a massive bloom, dead phytoplankton sink to the ocean or lake floor.
Then, decomposition occurs aided by bacteria.
This process lead to depletion of oxygen in the water, causing to suffocation of animals.
This may result to a massive death of aquatic organisms.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Bacillariophyta (Diatoms)

Bacillariophyta, commonly known as diatoms, are a major group of phytoplankton distinguished by their silica-based cell walls (frustules).
They inhabit diverse aquatic ecosystems including freshwater, brackish, and marine waters, as well as wetlands and moist soils.
Diatoms are ecologically critical, comprising a significant portion of marine phytoplankton and contributing up to 20–50% of global photosynthetic carbon fixation.
They exhibit diverse lifestyles: some are benthic (attached to substrates), while others are planktonic, and many possess a raphe—a specialized slit-like structure that enables active gliding motility over sediment surfaces.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics features of diatoms
1. Symmetry and Morphology



Diatoms are divided into two major groups based on symmetry:

Pennate diatoms
Centric diatoms

Pennate diatoms: Bilaterally symmetric (elongated, boat-shaped, or pen/needle-like).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Centric diatoms: Radially symmetric (circular, triangular, or cylindrical).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
2. Silica-based cell wall (frustule)



A cell wall of diatoms are characterised by having an external skeleton called frustule made of hydrated silica (\(SiO_2.nH_2O\)), which is like a box.
The lid of the box is called a valve and the sides are made of a series of bands called girdle.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
The frustule composed of a larger half called the epitheca fitting over a separate, slightly smaller half known as a hypotheca.

An epitheca – larger upper valve and
A hypotheca – smaller lower valve that fit together like a petri dish.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
The valve & bands are not solid silica, but have open structures
An individual opening is called areola
The areolae may be arranged in rows called striae

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: In centric diatoms the striae are oriented towards a point and in pennate diatoms towards a line.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
3. Locomotion

Pennate diatoms have a raphe, a slit-like structure that secretes mucopolysaccharides, allowing them to glide on surfaces.
Centric diatoms lack a raphe and are mostly non-motile, relying on water currents for movement.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
4. Pigments and photosynthesis

They possess chlorophyll-a & c (no chlorophyll b).
They also have fucoxanthin (a brown carotenoid) gives diatoms their golden-brown color.
They store energy as chrysolaminarin instead of starch.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
5. Reproduction

A dominant mode of reproduction is asexual reproduction by binary fission, where each daughter cell inherits one half of the frustule and regenerates the smaller half.
Over time, this leads to size reduction.
They also undergo sexual reproduction (size restoration):

Pennate diatoms: Auxospore formation (fusion of gametes to restore original size).
Centric diatoms: Oogamy (large non-motile egg + small motile sperm).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Classification of diatoms

The phylum Bacillariophyta (diatoms) is traditionally divided into two main classes, primarily based on symmetry and morphological features of their silica frustules;


Class bacillariophyceae (pennate diatoms)
Class coscinodiscophyceae (centric diatoms)

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
1. Class bacillariophyceae (pennate diatoms)

Body symmetry: – Bilateral (elongated, boat-shaped, or needle-like).
Many possess a raphe (slit for gliding motility).
Mostly benthic (attached to surfaces), but some planktonic.
Reproduction: Auxospores restore cell size after division.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
i). Order naviculales



They are bilaterally symmetric (elongated, boat-shaped
They possess a raphe (slit-like structure) for gliding motility.
Frustules have parallel striations (striae) and intricate pore patterns.
Examples: Navicula, Pinnularia.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text: Common genera of naviculales

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
ii). Order bacillariales



Order Bacillariales is a group of raphid pennate diatoms known for their elongated, needle-like or sigmoid (S-shaped) frustules and active motility.
They are found in both freshwater and marine environments.
Examples are Nitzschia, Hantzschia

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Characteristics of bacillariales
Morphology



They have an elongated, needle-like, or sigmoid (S-shaped) shape.
Some have keeled raphe systems (Nitzschia).
Unlike the central raphe of Naviculales, the keeled raphe is elevated on a raised ridge or keel along the edge of the valve
They have fine parallel lines (striae) or (pores) on the valve surface.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Motility

Raphe secretes mucopolysaccharides, enabling active gliding on surfaces.

This helps in escaping sedimentation, nutrient foraging, and biofilm formation.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Order bacillariales



Two genera;

Genus Nitzschia

text:
2. Class coscinodiscophyceae (centric diatoms)

They are composed of centric diatoms, characterized by radial symmetry and the absence of a raphe.
Five (5) subclasses


Subclass Coscinodiscophycidae
Subclass Rhizosoleniophycidae
Subclass Melosirophycidae
Subclass Thalassiosirophycidae
Subclass Biddulphiophycidae

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
1. Subclass Coscinodiscophycidae

Includes diatoms with predominantly radially symmetrical valves.
These are primarily marine planktonic species, though some occur in freshwater or benthic habitats.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Order Coscinodiscales



They are radial symmetry
They are found in marine environment

Common genera are Coscinodiscus, Hemidiscus

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology

They are star-like central structure

Found in marine

text:
2. Subclass Rhizosoleniophycidae

Consists of elongated, tubular, or needle-like diatoms with bilateral or radial symmetry.
Many are important oceanic plankton, contributing significantly to marine primary productivity.
Two orders:


Order rhizosoleniales
Order corethrales

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
Order rhizosoleniales



Their valve are elongated, needle-like shape (pennate-like but still centric).
They have bipolar or slightly radial body symmetry.
Often with elongated spines or extensions (e.g., “feet” in Rhizosolenia).
They have numerous, discoid or plate-like chloroplasts.
Found in marine (some form chains).

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:

text:

They have a cylindrical valve shape with distinctive spine-like projections.

Some species have long, hair-like setae.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:
3. Subclass Melosirophycidae

Consists of centric diatoms that often form chains or colonies.

title: AQ 224 – Plankton Systematics and Ecology
text:

Forms linear or zig-zag chains held together by organic threads or valve connections.
Have cylindrical or drum valve shape (discoid in some species).
Eg. Melosira
